title: Straddle as a Volatility Bet

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: A straddle = long call + long put at the SAME strike K and SAME maturity T on the SAME underlying. You pay c + p upfront.
rules: Payoff at maturity = max(S_T - K, 0) + max(K - S_T, 0) = |S_T - K|.
Profits if S_T moves far from K in EITHER direction.
Loses the premium if S_T ~ K at expiry.
setup.question: When would you buy a straddle?

learningObjectives: Recognize the absolute-value payoff of a straddle.
Identify the key trader motivation: realized vol > implied vol.
Understand why a straddle is NOT a pure vol bet (away from K, it accumulates directional exposure).

stages.label: Payoff
stages.prompt: Long call + long put, same K, same T. Payoff at expiry?

options.text: max(S_T - K, 0) + max(K - S_T, 0) = |S_T - K|.  V-shape centered at K.
0 if S_T near K, K otherwise.

stages.explanation: Exactly one of the two options finishes ITM (or both are zero at S_T = K). The combined payoff is the absolute deviation from strike.

stages.label: When to buy
stages.prompt: Why would you buy a straddle?

options.text: You expect a LARGE move in S but are AGNOSTIC about direction (e.g., earnings, FDA decision). Or: you believe realized vol will exceed implied vol.
You expect S_T to be close to K (so your delta exposure stays neutral).
You only profit if S_T < K.

stages.explanation: Straddles are used (i) for direction-agnostic event bets, (ii) as a (impure) volatility trade. ATM straddle price ~ 0.4 * sigma * S * sqrt(T), so it's roughly proportional to implied vol -- you buy if you think realized vol will exceed it.

stages.label: Commit -- limitation
stages.prompt: What's the main LIMITATION of a straddle as a pure volatility bet?

options.text: Once S moves far from K, the straddle's delta is no longer near 0 -- you accumulate directional exposure. For pure vol bets, use variance / volatility swaps.
Straddles can only be sold, not bought.
There is no limitation -- a straddle is a pure vol bet.

stages.explanation: AT inception, an ATM straddle has delta ~ 0 (call delta = 0.5, put delta = -0.5). But as S drifts away, the call's delta rises and the put's falls in absolute terms, so net delta becomes nonzero. Variance swaps (paying realized variance) sidestep this entirely.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What's the breakeven? (Price moves of magnitude >= c + p in either direction.)
What is a STRANGLE and how does it differ from a straddle? (Strangle = long OTM call + long OTM put at different strikes; cheaper but needs a bigger move to profit.)
How does a variance swap pay off? (N * (sigma_realized^2 - K_var), pure vol exposure with no directional component.)
